stories.headline: Nuclear facilities targeted on both sides as Iran war escalates
stories.body: The Iran war entered dangerous new territory on Saturday as the US and Israel struck Iran's Natanz nuclear enrichment facility, one of the country's most important uranium sites, while Iran retaliated by firing missiles at the Israeli town of Dimona, home to Israel's nuclear research center. Iran's atomic energy organisation said no radioactive materials leaked from Natanz, and the IAEA confirmed no increase in off-site radiation levels. In Dimona, at least 39 people were injured including a ten-year-old child. Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz warned that strikes on Iran would intensify significantly in the coming week, while Trump hinted at potentially winding down the conflict. The war has now lasted 22 days, with the US claiming to have struck 8,000 military targets across Iran.

stories.headline: Iran fires missiles at US-UK military base on Diego Garcia
stories.body: Iran targeted the joint US-UK military base on Diego Garcia in the Indian Ocean with two ballistic missiles, according to reports from the Wall Street Journal and CNN. One missile reportedly failed in flight while the other was intercepted by a US warship, with neither reaching its target. UK Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper denounced the attack as a reckless escalation but insisted Britain would not be drawn into a wider offensive conflict, saying the UK would only provide defensive support. The incident raised questions about Iran's missile range capabilities, as Diego Garcia lies approximately 2,350 miles from Iranian territory. The UK had recently agreed to let the US use British bases for strikes on Iranian sites threatening the Strait of Hormuz.

stories.headline: EU urges early gas storage as Iran war drives energy prices higher
stories.body: The European Commission urged member states to begin filling gas reserves immediately after Iranian attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure sent prices surging. Energy Commissioner Dan Jorgensen wrote to member states asking them to consider lowering their winter storage target to 80 percent to ease pressure during the filling period. The move followed Iran's attack on Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG complex, which knocked out 17 percent of Doha's export capacity, with repairs expected to take up to five years. Natural gas prices in the EU have risen more than 30 percent since the war began on February 28, and oil prices have soared over 50 percent. A separate analysis found the war has already produced 5 million tonnes of CO2 emissions in its first 14 days, draining the global carbon budget faster than 84 countries combined.

stories.headline: At least 14 killed in factory fire in South Korea
stories.body: A fire at a car parts factory in Daejeon, South Korea, killed at least 14 workers and injured nearly 60. About 170 workers were inside the three-storey building when the blaze broke out on Friday afternoon. The fire spread so rapidly that workers began jumping from windows before firefighters arrived. Responders were initially delayed because sodium stored at the site could explode on contact with water, requiring removal of more than 200 kilograms of reactive chemicals before hoses could be used. Nine of the dead were found in a third-floor gym locker room. The factory, owned by Anjun Industrial, is a parts supplier for Hyundai and Kia. It is the deadliest factory fire in South Korea since 23 workers died at a lithium battery plant in 2024.

stories.headline: Suspense in French municipal elections as Paris, Marseille and Lyon vote
stories.body: Voters in France's three largest cities headed to the polls in a closely watched second round of municipal elections on Sunday, with outcomes in Paris, Marseille and Lyon all uncertain. In Paris, socialist candidate Emmanuel Gregoire, who served as deputy to outgoing mayor Anne Hidalgo, led conservative rival Rachida Dati by just two points in polls. Dati received a boost after the far-right Reconquista party withdrew to help end 25 years of left-wing rule in the capital, and President Macron is widely seen as backing her candidacy despite officially remaining neutral. Dati faces a corruption trial in September. The elections are seen as a barometer of national political sentiment ahead of the next presidential race.

stories.headline: Robert Mueller dies at 81; Trump gloats, drawing bipartisan condemnation
stories.body: Robert Mueller, the former FBI director and special counsel whose investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 election defined much of Trump's first term, died on Friday at 81. Mueller served as FBI director from 2001 to 2013 and was a decorated Vietnam War veteran who earned a Bronze Star and Purple Heart. His death prompted an extraordinary response from Trump, who wrote on Truth Social that he was glad Mueller was dead and that he could no longer hurt innocent people. The comment drew condemnation from both Democrats and Republicans, with former RNC chairman Michael Steele calling Trump a vile, disgusting man and multiple senators calling the president's response despicable and unfit for office.

stories.headline: Trump threatens to deploy ICE agents to airports amid DHS shutdown
stories.body: President Trump announced he would order Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents to take over airport security duties starting Monday unless Democrats agreed to fund the Department of Homeland Security. The threat came as a partial government shutdown caused long screening lines at major airports, with TSA officers working without pay. Trump framed the move as an immigration enforcement expansion, saying ICE agents would arrest all illegal immigrants at airports, with a particular focus on Somali immigrants. Democrats have been blocking DHS funding over demands for reforms to immigration enforcement following a Minnesota operation in which two protesters were fatally shot, asking for better identification requirements and a new code of conduct for federal agents.

stories.headline: Hawaii suffers worst flooding in 20 years as dam threatens to fail
stories.body: More than 5,500 people were evacuated as Hawaii endured its worst flooding in two decades, with authorities warning a 120-year-old dam on Oahu could fail. More than 230 people were rescued as flash floods inundated streets, swept away vehicles and lifted homes in communities north of Honolulu. Some areas received more than 10 inches of rainfall with winds reaching 100 miles per hour. Governor Josh Green said there were no reported fatalities but warned the storm caused enormous damage to airports, schools, roads and hospitals, with estimated costs potentially exceeding one billion dollars. It was the second severe storm to hit the archipelago in a week.

stories.headline: Wyoming signs six-week abortion ban as Trump administration targets state abortion coverage
stories.body: Wyoming's governor signed a ban on abortions after approximately six weeks of pregnancy while expressing misgivings about the law's strictness. Separately, the Trump administration launched investigations into states that mandate health insurance coverage for abortion, and a judge in Arizona struck down old abortion restrictions that clashed with voter-approved constitutional guarantees. The moves came alongside a Nevada case in which a woman who was convicted under a 1911 law after suffering a miscarriage received a 100,000 dollar settlement from the state. Abortion access continued to be a major legal and political battleground across multiple states.

stories.headline: Double gender violence tragedy: girl killed in Torrevieja, woman shot dead in Zaragoza
stories.body: Spain was shaken by two gender violence killings on Saturday. In Torrevieja, Alicante, a three-year-old girl was killed by her father in what investigators are treating as vicarious violence, in which a parent harms children to punish the other parent. The mother had gone to police late Friday night to report death threats against their daughter, but when officers arrived at the father's home they found both him and the child dead. Separately in Zaragoza, a man shot a woman dead in the street before taking his own life. The government's delegate in Valencia called vicarious violence an atrocious form of terrorism and urged society not to treat gender violence as a private family matter.

stories.headline: Government boosts defense spending 25% this year without congressional approval
stories.body: The Spanish government has increased defense spending by more than 25 percent so far in 2026, reaching 2.135 billion euros through executive mechanisms that bypass Congress, where it lacks a majority. The largest allocation was 1.339 billion euros approved on March 10 via a transfer from the Finance Ministry. The latest spending, approved this week, included 29.7 million euros for anti-drone defense systems for navy ships. The increases come amid the Iran war and separately from a 1 billion euro bilateral military aid commitment to Ukraine announced during President Zelenski's visit to Madrid. Coalition partner Izquierda Unida criticized the Ukraine spending as prolonging the war.

stories.headline: Storm Therese batters Canary Islands with floods and flight cancellations
stories.body: Storm Therese hit the Canary Islands with heavy rain, causing 24 flight cancellations, landslides and flooding alerts. Gran Canaria declared a Level 1 emergency and urged residents to avoid travel. A migrant boat carrying more than 100 people also arrived at the port of Los Cristianos in Tenerife during the storm. Meanwhile on the mainland, the government's two crisis decrees responding to the Iran war were published in the official gazette on Saturday, entering into force Sunday. The measures include reducing VAT on electricity, gas and fuel to 10 percent, capping rent increases at 2 percent, and extending energy subsidies for vulnerable households through the end of 2026.

stories.headline: Junts set to block government's housing decree on rent controls
stories.body: The Catalan independence party Junts announced it would vote against the government's housing decree that caps rent increases at 2 percent until late 2027, saying the measure suffocates small landlords. The decree, championed by junior coalition partner Sumar after a dramatic standoff with the PSOE in Friday's cabinet meeting, faces a congressional vote on Thursday. Sumar also announced it would hold an assembly after the Andalusian elections to elect new leadership and redefine its identity following Yolanda Diaz's departure. The political turbulence comes as Podemos has separately said it will not support the government's main crisis decree unless Spain exits NATO.

stories.headline: Man shot dead at Badalona karaoke bar; Mossos arrest 87 repeat offenders in crackdown
stories.body: A 38-year-old man was fatally shot at a karaoke bar in Badalona's La Salut neighbourhood in the early hours of Saturday morning, with a second person wounded but expected to survive. The killing occurred during a large police operation as the Mossos d'Esquadra were conducting a new edition of their Pla Kanpai crackdown on repeat offenders across the Barcelona metropolitan area. The broader operation resulted in 87 arrests of individuals with a combined 293 prior police records, along with 29 criminal investigations and 232 administrative citations. In Barcelona alone, police identified 1,063 people with 2,330 combined prior offences and seized around 20 weapons.

stories.headline: Unvaccinated Catalan teenager in ICU with tetanus
stories.body: A 17-year-old whose family refuses vaccination was admitted to intensive care in a Catalan hospital in very serious condition with a tetanus infection. The teenager had suffered a fracture in a fall about two weeks ago, and after being treated and sent home, developed neurological symptoms including spasms and muscle rigidity. Tetanus is a serious but entirely preventable disease caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani entering the body through wounds. The case has reignited debate about vaccine refusal, with medical authorities noting that doctors could seek a court order to prioritize treatment if the family continues to reject it, given the grave risk to the minor.

stories.headline: Hermit ibis released in Emporda wetlands after 400-year absence
stories.body: Nineteen hermit ibis were released into the wild at the Aiguamolls de l'Emporda natural park in the Alt Emporda, marking the return of the critically endangered species to Catalan skies after a 400-year absence. The birds, fitted with GPS tracking devices, had been raised in captivity and cared for by volunteers who wore black clothing and helmets shaped like ibis heads so the birds would see them as adoptive parents. The Emporda colony is one of just three reintroduction sites in Europe, alongside populations in the northern Alps and Andalusia. Catalonia's ecological transition secretary called the release a moment of reconciliation with nature and correcting a historical human error.

stories.headline: Cap Butaca Buida theater festival sets attendance record
stories.body: The third edition of Cap Butaca Buida, a festival promoting theater attendance across Catalonia, set a new record with more than 81,000 spectators filling venues in a single day of celebration. The initiative, which offers reduced-price tickets to encourage people to experience live theater, brought audiences to dozens of venues across Barcelona and Catalonia. The event coincided with the run-up to World Theatre Day and included special programming at venues including La Puntual, which offered performances aimed at younger audiences to cultivate the next generation of theatergoers.
